coredata.dc:description: The traditional chlorine bleaching technique produces highly toxic tetrachloroguaiacol (TeCG), a chemical that not only poses a serious threat to aquatic organisms and disrupts the food chain in the ecosystem, but also causes a series of health problems. In order to remove TeCG from wastewater, a novel Fenton catalyst was developed in this study, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and cysteine as raw materials, and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs were prepared by reconstituting calcined Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs using GQDs. simulation experiments were conducted to verify that the The key role of GQDs in TeCG degradation: the introduction of GQDs significantly increased the specific surface area and dispersibility of the catalysts, and meanwhile accelerated the charge transfer on the catalyst surface, enhanced the separation efficiency of charge carriers, and enhanced the degradation efficiency of the pollutants. The results showed that the maximum removal rate of the catalyst for 10 mg/L TeCG could reach 89.34 % within 60 min, and the catalyst maintained high stability after several cycles. According to the gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis, the decomposition of TeCG was mainly converted to low-toxicity or non-toxicity products through oxidative dechlorination and aryl ring cleavage processes. The multiphase Fenton catalyst reconstituted by GQDs proposed in this paper has the ability to remove chlorinated pollutants with high efficiency, which shows a broad application prospect in the field of wastewater treatment and provides important theoretical and experimental support for the development of efficient Fenton catalysts.

(a) TEM of GQDs, inset shows particle size distribution (7 ± 0.67 nm) (b) HETEM and lattice spacing of GQDs, (c) TEM of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, (d) TEM of Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs, (e) TEM of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs, inset is a SEAD plot, (f) HETEM and laminar structure analysis of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs (g) PL spectra of GQDs vs. LDHs/GQDs (h) AFM images of GQDs (Average thickness 1.72 nm) (i) AFM images of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs(Average thickness 12.35 nm).

(a-e) Kinetic analysis of TeCG degradation under different experimental conditions and (f) Activation energy of TeCG catalysed degradation reaction (Conditions: Adsorption time = 20 min, [TeCG]0 = 10 mg·L-1, [H2O2]0 = 10 mmol/L, initial pH = 3.0, illumination intensity = 1700 μW·cm−2, T = 45 °C).
Two postulated pathways for TeCG transformation (Path1:oxidative dechlorination Path2: aryl ring cleavage).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The traditional chlorine bleaching technique produces highly toxic tetrachloroguaiacol (TeCG), a chemical that not only poses a serious threat to aquatic organisms and disrupts the food chain in the ecosystem, but also causes a series of health problems. In order to remove TeCG from wastewater, a novel Fenton catalyst was developed in this study, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and cysteine as raw materials, and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs were prepared by reconstituting calcined Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs using GQDs. simulation experiments were conducted to verify that the The key role of GQDs in TeCG degradation: the introduction of GQDs significantly increased the specific surface area and dispersibility of the catalysts, and meanwhile accelerated the charge transfer on the catalyst surface, enhanced the separation efficiency of charge carriers, and enhanced the degradation efficiency of the pollutants. The results showed that the maximum removal rate of the catalyst for 10 mg/L TeCG could reach 89.34 % within 60 min, and the catalyst maintained high stability after several cycles. According to the gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis, the decomposition of TeCG was mainly converted to low-toxicity or non-toxicity products through oxidative dechlorination and aryl ring cleavage processes. The multiphase Fenton catalyst reconstituted by GQDs proposed in this paper has the ability to remove chlorinated pollutants with high efficiency, which shows a broad application prospect in the field of wastewater treatment and provides important theoretical and experimental support for the development of efficient Fenton catalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Many pulp and paper mills in China still use conventional technology for chlorine bleaching processing, resulting inlarge amounts of chlorinated organic pollutants in bleaching effluents effluents [1,2]. Among them, chlorinated phenols are a significant class of environmental pollutants due to their persistence and bioaccumulation in the environment. Additionally, they are highly toxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic to aquatic life and humans [3]. Highly substituted chlorinated methoxyphenols, such as tetrachloroguaiacol (TeCG), are the most toxic compounds with very high concentrations in bleach effluents [4]. The efficient removal of TeCG has been less studied in existing research, therefore, developing highly efficient degradants for degrading highly hazardous tetrachloroguaiacol (TeCG) before discharging the bleaching effluents into the environment is crucial and urgent.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), with a general formula of [M2+ 1-
x M3+
x (OH)2][An-] x
/n·zH2O, are widely used in the studies of catalyzed organic pollutants owing to their excellent properties, including high specific surface area, environmental friendliness, lower cost, and non-toxicity [5,6]. In addition, LDHs and their derivatives have attracted considerable attention from researcher owing to their layered structure and compositional variability [7–10]. Li [11] found that Mg/Al/Fe-CLDH (calcined Mg/Al/Fe-LDH) exhibited extremely high arsenate removal efficiency over a wide pH range (2–12), suggesting that trimetallic Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs are a promising multiphase catalyst. Meanwhile, LDHs can be used to construct metal-hydroxide interfacial sites owing to their memory effect. Compared with pristine LDHs, the rehydrated LDHs can exhibit different properties, electronic structure, and defect structure due to the missing surface hydroxyl groups and the replacement of interlayered anion groups [5]. Zhang [12] synthesized rehydrated MgAl-LDHs-supported Pt nanoparticles. The obtained composites exhibited excellent catalytic glycerol performance due to the interfacial Ptδ+ species formed through the electronic interaction of the Pt-LDH interface. Thus, the reconstruction of LDHs may promote their catalytic activity.

Combining LDHs with carbon nanomaterials can improve the catalytic performance of LDHs. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are novel carbon-based nanomaterials with oxygenated multifunctional groups. GQDs are notable for their ability to be directly prepared and produced from inexpensive, sustainable materials. Additionally, they exhibit favorable characteristics such as high chemical reactivity, easily functionalized, low toxicity, biocompatibility, small particle size, and large surface area [13]. GQDs can eliminate inorganic and organic waste through their oxygenated functional groups, which bind metal ions from aqueous solutions [14]. A recent study demonstrated the enhanced efficacy of modified GQDs combined with graphitic carbon nitride for the photocatalytic elimination of antibiotics [15]. Further studies showed that the use of ZIF-8@GQD eliminated malachite green (MG) solution [16]. Another study also examined a polyvinyl chloride membrane infused with GQDs to eliminate dye from wastewater [17]. Researchers have successfully attached GQDs onto the NiFe2O4-halloysite to remove Cd (II) ions [18]. Therefore, functionalized GQDs are promising materials for removing contaminants from wastewater.

GQDs are characterized by abundant oxygen groups, more active sites, and rich edges. These unique characteristics increase the number of active centers and effectively prevent ionic aggregation within the LDHs [19]. In addition, the LDHs can electrostatically interact with the GQDs, thereby enhancing the stability of the material. Therefore, investigating the combined effect of GQDs and LDHs for synthesizing a nanocomposite with enhanced chemical adsorption properties is crucial. The combined technique aims to improve the presence of specific functional groups by using GQDs to reconstruct Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs composites, thereby enhancing catalytic activity and stability in the Fenton-like degradation of TeCG. To the best of our knowledge, only a few studies on composites of GQDs reconstructed LDHs have been reported, for example, Guo [20] uses GQDs to modulate the electronic structure on the surface of NiFe-LDHs to enhance the catalytic activity of the OER reaction and improve the performance of Zn-air cells; Mohamed [21] utilised GQDs coupled with FeAl-LDHand intercalated by co-precipitation with Gemifloxacin antibiotic (Gem) for effectively removing Pb(II) (90.7 %). However, the application of such catalysts in TeCG degradation has not yet been reported.

In this study, GQDs-reconstituted magnetic hydrotalcite composites (Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs) were prepared via the hydrothermal method and calcination–rehydration cycle. Then, they were used as heterogeneous Fenton reagents for the degradation of TeCG. The effects of different Fenton systems, Fe content, H2O2 concentration, initial solution pH and temperature on the degradation of TeCG were investigated and discussed in detail. In addition, GC–MS was used to determine the final degradation products of TeCG and to deduce the possible degradation mechanisms. The resulting Fenton-like catalysts, Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs, exhibited superior catalytic activity and stability in the degradation of TeCG.

Magnesium nitrate hexahydrate, aluminium nitrate nonhydrate, ferric chloride hexahydrate, urea, concentrated sulphuric acid, cysteine, citric acid, deionised water, tetrachloroguaiacol, anhydrous ethanol and hydrogen peroxide were purchased from Sinopec Group Chemical Reagent Co.

The Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs composites were prepared as follows [11,22]: 0.3456 g of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.1519 g of Al(NO3)3·9H2O, and 0.122 g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 30 mL of deionised water to prepare a mixed cationic salt solution, and then 30 mL of 19.8 mM/mol urea solution was added into the mixed cationic salt solution by using a constant flow pump under magnetic stirring. The solution was stirred for 30 min and then transferred to a Teflon lined reactor and heated in an oven at 160 °C for 24 h to obtain Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs. The Fe content of the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs varies and can be obtained by adjusting the amount of FeCl3·6H2O added.

A 0.5 g sample of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs was milled and placed in a tube furnace, set at a temperature increase rate of 10 °C/min, and calcined in a stream of 5 % hydrogen at 450 °C for 2 h. As the temperature continued to increase, the hydroxyl groups were further removed, and the lamellar structure was disrupted, resulting in the formation of amorphous mixed metal oxides.

Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (NS-GQDs) were prepared by the same method as previously reported [23]. Specifically, 1 g of citric acid and 2 g of l-cysteine were dissolved in 5 ml of deionised water and sonicated for 2 h to obtain a completely clarified solution. The solution was then transferred to a 20 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, heated to 200 °C and maintained for 3 h. The resulting brown suspension was filtered using a 0.2 μm membrane and dialysed using a dialysis membrane with a 1000 retention for 24 h. The solution was then concentrated to 5 mL and freeze-dried.

Then 0.2 g of calcined Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs was added to 50 ml of decarboxamide solvent and the mixture was stirred under nitrogen for 8 h to completely dissolve the Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs into a clear, transparent system. At the end of the reaction, the sample was cooled, centrifuged, washed until impurities were removed, and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h. Finally, the resulting product was ground to obtain magnetic Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/ recombined from GQDs, called magnetic Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs.

A typical degradation experiment was performed by adding 50 mL of a 10 mg·L-1 solution of tetrachloroguaiacol (TeCG) to a 100 mL catalytic reactor. 0.1 g of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs was added and stirred for 20 min to reach adsorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of 80 mmol·L-1 H2O2 was added to the reaction solution to initiate the degradation reaction. Timing was started and at a fixed interval (10 min), 2 ml of reaction solution was extracted with a filter membrane and the reaction was immediately quenched by adding 100 μL of ethanol. The absorbance of TeCG was measured by UV spectrophotometer at 296 nm and compared with the standard curve (Fig.S1) to determine the concentration of TeCG. To ensure the reliability of the test results, all experiments were performed three times and the average values were calculated.

The removal efficiency was calculated by using Eq. (1):

Where C and C0 are the concentration at a given time and the initial concentration, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the spectrum of X-ray diffraction of GQDs shows a broad diffraction peak centred at 26.2°, which corresponds to the (002) crystal face of GQDs [24,25], suggesting that the GQDs have a disordered graphene structure. The diffraction peaks of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs samples at 11.6°, 23.5°, 35.0°, 39.6°, 47.1°, 60.9°, and 62.3° were ascribed to the (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110), and (113) planes of LDHs, respectively, which corresponded to the standard LDHs data (PDF#35–0965), indicating that the material was successfully prepared. Comparing the XRD patterns of Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs with those of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, we observed that the characteristic peaks of LDHs disappeared at 42.8° and appeared at 62.3°. MIIO and MIIMIII 2O4 spinel was formed. The MIIO crystalline phase was consistently observed when the molar ratio of MII/MIII in the spinel was 3:1. The appearance of diffraction peaks caused by the spinel crystals mainly depends on the properties of the metal cations and the calcining temperature. Mg/Al spinel crystalline phases were detected, suggesting that the hydrocalcined products of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs are mainly MgO. Meanwhile, Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ during the hydrogen calcination process, yielding the Fe3O4 phase [26]. The diffraction pattern of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs revealed that LDHs were reconstructed, as reflected by the reappearance of the characteristic peaks of LDHs. The diffraction also revealed that the introduction of GQDs did not change the layered structure of the hydrotalcite. The diffraction peaks of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs were sharp and symmetrical, indicating that the prepared using the structural reconstruction method composites exhibited high crystallinity. Based on the (003) reflection, the interlayer spacing of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs can be calculated as 0.758 nm.

Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectra of four samples. In the spectra of these four samples, the strong broad absorption peak near 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H vibrational absorption in the –COOH group and the water molecule group [27]. In the FTIR spectra of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, the strong absorption peak near 1357 cm−1 was attributed to the asymmetric vibrations of the interlayer CO3 2– anions [28]. The vibrational peaks at 887 cm−1 and 742 cm−1 were attributed to oxygen-metal–oxygen stretching vibrations, whereas the peak appearing at 630 cm−1 was attributed to the characteristic bending vibrations of the metal–oxygen (M−O) bond in the LDHs [29]. The FTIR spectra of the calcined Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs were almost the same as the original ones, but the M−O bond peak at 630 cm−1 disappeared, which was speculated to be probably due to the reduction of M−O by oxygen as a result of calcination. Compared with the FTIR spectra of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, a new absorption peak near 1609 cm−1 was observed in the FTIR spectra of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. The new peak was attributed to the stretching vibration of the carbon–carbon bond (C=C/C-C) and the carbon skeleton of GQDs [30]. The new peak located at 1361.8 cm−1 was attributed to the C-OH and C-O-C stretching vibrations, demonstrating successful intercalation of GQDs in Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs.

The Raman spectra of GQDs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs are shown in Fig. 1c. The peak at 1360 cm−1 was attributed to the D-band of the carbon material and the G-band of the carbon material at 1600 cm−1 [31]. The calculated value of the intensity ratio of the D-band to the G-band (ID/IG) indicates the degree of disorder in the carbon material, which is inversely proportional to the size of the sp2 structural domain of the carbon. The values of ID/IG in the Raman spectra of GQDs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs were 0.79 and 0.92, respectively. The increased values of ID/IG for Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs indicate that the interaction of LDHs with GQDs produces a more disordered structure in the carbon material [32]. The decrease in the average size of the sp2 structural domain increased the ID/IG value, where LDHs removed oxygen groups from GQDs during their growth, resulting in some unpaired defects [33].

XPS measurements characterised the Fe(II) and Fe(III) content in the three materials (Fig. 1d, Fig. S2). The absence of the typical Fe2O3 core level peak at about 718 eV and the large increase in the values of Fe(II)/Fe(III) in Mg/Al/ Fe-LDOs (0.5772) compared to Mg/Al/ Fe-LDHs (0.3267) according to the Fe 2p spectra (Fig. S2) suggests that in Mg/Al/ Fe-LDOs of Fe(III) was reduced to Fe(II) by H2.The Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peaks of FeO are located at about 723 eV and 710 eV, respectively.The Fe(II): Fe(III) = 0.5672 in Mg/Al/ Fe-LDHs/GQDs is close to the Fe(II): Fe(III) = 0.5384, indicating that the synthesised samples are mixed valence Fe3O4 [34]. The characteristic peaks of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 of mixed valence iron oxides are located at 724.9 eV and 711.5 eV, respectively, and are shifted towards higher binding energies than the standard peaks, as shown in Fig. 1d. This phenomenon may be due to the change of electron density around the iron atoms during the reduction process. This result indicates that the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHS/GQDs were loaded with magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles by hydrogen reduction.

The nanostructural morphology of the various materials was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 2 ). In Fig. 2a, the synthesised GQDs show partial agglomeration, which is more related to the high water absorption of GQDs, and the agglomeration will cover the catalytic site of the reaction, which is not conducive to the catalytic reaction. In Fig. 2b, the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs showed a characteristic layer structure similar to that of hydrotalcite, which proved that the synthesis of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs was successful. After the modification, the GQDs were uniformly distributed on the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, and this structure helped to increase the specific surface area of the materials, facilitated the charge transfer and accelerated the catalytic reaction. The elemental distribution in the materials was analyzed using EDX, and the results showed that the elements C, N, O, Mg, Al, Fe were uniformly distributed in the catalyst. In summary, Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs with heterojunction were successfully synthesised.

Fig. 3 a and b show the TEM and HETEM of GQDs, respectively, and the inset shows the particle size distribution of GQDs. From Fig. 3a, it can be seen that the GQDs are spherical and the prepared GQDs are not well dispersed, they appear slightly agglomerated. HETEM (Fig. 3a) shows a lattice stripe of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) face of GQDs. Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d show the TEM images of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs before and after calcination, respectively, and their structures remain basically the same. The GQDs can be seen distributed in Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs in Fig. 3e, and the two lattice spacings can also be seen crossing each other to form a layered structure in the HETEM [35] (Fig. 3f). The SEAD plot shows wider diffuse Debye loops, indicating that the nanoparticles have formed on the LDH and are randomly orientated with smaller particle sizes. In summary, Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs with heterojunction were successfully synthesized.

PL spectra (Fig. 3g) showed that NS-GQDs exhibited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (420 nm). Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs show weaker blue light at 438 nm. The thickness change of the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs material before and after loading of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs was investigated using AFM (Fig. S3, Fig. S4). The layer height analysis revealed that the thickness of the GQDs (Fig. S3) averaged about 1.3 nm, indicating that the GQDs should be a monolayer nanostructure rather than a multilayer mechanism [36]. The increase in the average thickness of the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs material (Fig. S4) indicated that the loading of the LDHs resulted in an increase in the thickness of the composite material, which proved that the two materials were synthesised together successfully.

The thermogravimetric-differential thermal analysis (TG-DTA) technique was conducted on both Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs to analyze their thermal stability. As shown in Fig. S5, the main weight loss of the samples occurred in two stages. In the first stage, the desorption of surface-adsorbed water and intercrystalline water molecules occurred below 240 °C for the two samples. Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs exhibited a lower weight loss rate (9 %) than Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs (16 %). In the second stage, weight loss of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs occurred at a wider temperature range (240–580 °C) compared with that of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs (240–470 °C). In addition, Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs exhibited a higher weight loss rate (38 %) than Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs (31 %), indicating that intercalation of GQDs led to higher thermal decomposition temperature of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. The rise in the temperature occurred because the increased interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups and layered structure made the LDHs more thermally stable.

The hysteresis loops of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. The saturation magnetisation Ms of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs are 0.957 emu/g and 25.15 emu/g, respectively.. These results showed that when Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs were calcined at 450 °C, some of Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ by hydrogen. Fe3O4 was detected in the mixed metal oxide formed by calcination, consistent with the results of the XRD analysis. The saturation magnetization Ms of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs was 5.67 emu/g, and their magnetic properties were much weaker than the mixed metal oxide Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the recovery and reconstruction of the collapsed structure of Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs. The initially exposed magnetic Fe3O4 was wrapped in the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs metal laminate. The charge on the laminate shielded some of the magnetic properties, decreasing the saturation magnetization of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs.

Fig. 4b shows the zero field cooling curve for Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. The curve shows that the inflection point temperature is 69 K, which belongs to the Neel temperature ΘN. Below 69 K, the magnetic susceptibility 1/χM decreases with increasing temperature. This phenomenon indicates that when the temperature is lower than 69 K, χM increases with increasing temperature, demonstrating antiferromagnetic properties of the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs at low temperatures. When the temperature is higher than the Neel temperature, the magnetic susceptibility 1/χM increases as the temperature increases, indicating an inverse relationship between χM and the temperature, which demonstrates its paramagnetic properties.

Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d show nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves and pore size distributions of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry classification, these two isotherms belong to type IV and H3 hysteresis loops, indicating that mesoporous structures exist in both materials and are formed by mesoporous packing structures of hydrotalcite two-dimensional plate crystals. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to calculate the specific surface area of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs, which was 104.81 m2/g, much larger than that of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs (34.7468 m2/g). The pore size (3.73–3.98 nm) of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs was less than that of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs (19.72–19.83 nm). These findings show that the intercalation of GQDs can reduce the aggregation between Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs, resulting in a more uniform dispersion of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs nanosheets. The uniformly dispersed Mg/Al/Fe-LDH nanosheets reduce the pore size and increase the specific surface area of the material, thereby improving the adsorption efficiency [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different catalysts on TeCG degradation
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 5 a, it can be seen that the removal of TeCG by each catalyst system follows an increasing trend with time, and the reaction time gradually reaches a plateau after 60 min, which may be the depletion of the internal active sites or free radicals. Therefore, 60 min was chosen as the reaction time for a series of degradation experiments.

In order to evaluate the catalytic efficiency of the materials, the degradation of TeCG was evaluated using three materials activated with H2O2. As shown in Fig. 5a, among the three different TeCG degradation systems, Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs showed the highest removal of TeCG, reaching 84.68 % in 60 min, while Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs and Mg/Al/Fe-LDOs showed 19.01 % and 57.81 % removal of TeCG in 60 min, respectively. The removal of TeCG by Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs had fewer active sites and lower activation efficiency of H2O2, leading to the removal effect decreasing. Some of the Fe3+ in the Mg/Al/Fe-LDO was reduced to Fe2+ after calcination with hydrogen, and the formation of Fe3O4 also acted as the active site of Fenton reaction, which increased the number of active sites, expanded the opportunity of pollutants binding with the active sites, and the catalytic efficiency could be improved [38]. After the reconfiguration of the GQDs, the removal rate of TeCG by Mg /Al/Fe-LDHs catalyst has a large specific surface area and high dispersibility, in addition, the GQDs intercalation also accelerated the electron transport and facilitated the conversion between Fe3+ and Fe2+, the findings show these properties can accelerate the efficiency of activating H2O2 and generate more HO• to participate in the reaction, thus realizing the high efficiency of TeCG removal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of Fe content on TeCG removal rate
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5b, the removal efficiency of TeCG increased with increasing Fe/(Fe + Al) molar ratio in the catalyst Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. When the molar ratio of Fe/(Fe + Al) was 0.4, the TeCG removal efficiency of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs reached the maximum value of 89.34 %. This high TeCG removal efficiency may be ascribed to the high content of Fe3O4 in the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs catalyst.

Nano-Fe3O4 is a good Fenton reagent with catalytic activity similar to peroxide mimetic enzymes. Its catalytic properties are consistent with those of natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), which catalyzes the formation of highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (HO•) and O2 from H2O2 [39].

In the heterogeneous Fenton system, the metal active centers are mainly fixed on the GQDs intercalated LDHs. Due to the electron transport of GQDs, an inhomogeneous distribution of electron density was formed. The Fe3O4 particles located on GQDs and the Fe3O4 particles dispersed on layers of LDHs tend to form activedual-reactioncenters, representing the rich and poor electronics centers, respectively. The TeCG and H2O2 were clustered around the electron-rich center and electron-poor center, respectively. In the catalytic process, the electron-rich center located on GQDs absorbed a large amount of H2O2 and rapidly reduced H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals HO•, while the electron-poor center dispersed on layers of LDHs adsorbed TeCG and intermediates, and then transferred the electrons provided by the TeCG to the electron-rich center [40]. In addition, GQDs exhibited a high specific surface area, facilitating the adsorption and activation of polluting molecules [41]. GQDs are visible light responsive, which can effectively separate the photogenerated electrons and holes, reduce the complexation of electrons and holes, and improve the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction [42]. Therefore, intercalation of GQDs into the LDHs layer can effectively improve the photocatalytic activity and hydrogen peroxide utilization efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of H2O2 concentration on TeCG removal rate
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of H2O2 addition on TeCG removal was investigated. The catalyst can activate H2O2 to generate HO•, which can promote the oxidative decomposition of pollutants. As shown in Fig. 5c, within the H2O2 dose range of 20–60 mmol·L-1, the removal and degradation efficiency of TeCG increased with increasing H2O2 concentration. The appropriate amount of H2O2 may provide more HO•, facilitating the degradation of TeCG.

When the concentration of H2O2 was 60 mmol/L, the removal rate of TeCG by Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs reached the maximum. However, when the H2O2 concentration continued to increase, both the removal and degradation rates decreased significantly. It is speculated that the reason may be that the excessive H2O2 concentration may oxidize the active sites in the catalyst, which may hinder the catalytic reaction and lead to occurrence of side reactions during the reaction [43], therefore reduces the service life of the catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on TeCG removal rate
ce:section.ce:para: pH is one of the important parameters affecting the Fenton reaction, where Fe generates HO• with H2O2 under acidic conditions with the reaction equation: Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3++ HO• + OH–. As shown in Fig. 5d, the removal and degradation of TeCG increased with increasing pH, reached a maximum value of 87.56 % at pH 3.0, and decreased with increasing pH over 3. This phenomenon indicates that an acidic environment promotes the release of electrons and Fe2+ from Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. The ph0 (Fig. S6), of the material was 4. 75, when the pH in the solution was higher than 4.75, the surface of the material was negatively charged, inhibiting the degradation efficiency. [Fe(II)(H2O)]2+ was easily formed at pH < 3.0 and reacted with H2O2 to reduce the formation of HO•, decreasing the degradation rate of TeCG.When the pH exceeded 3.0, iron ions in the solution precipitated, thus inhibiting the decomposition of H2O2 and reducing the amount of HO• production, thereby inhibiting the degradation of the contaminant [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of temperature on TeCG removal rate
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5e, the effect of temperature on the removal rate of TeCG was investigated, from which it can be seen that the removal efficiency of TeCG by the catalyst increased as the temperature increased from 15 °C to 45 °C, reaching a maximum value of 86.39 % at 45 °C. The increase in temperature exacerbated the molecular thermal motion, and made the TeCG molecules collide effectively with the catalyst or other activated molecules, on the other hand, the increase in temperature could lead to an increase in the degree of decomposition of H2O2, and promote the generation of more active radicals. However, as the temperature continued to rise to 55 °C, the removal of TeCG by the catalyst decreased instead, the finding should be attributed to the increase in temperature, thehigher temperature led to the rapid oxidation of iron, making the Fenton reaction of the catalyst weakened, so the overall removal rate decreased. Therefore, the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs degradation of TeCG system can have the most excellent effect at 45 °C.

The stability and reusability of the catalyst are significant for its potential application. As shown in Fig. 5f, the degradation rate of TeCG decreased slightly with the increase in the number of reuse. The catalysts still maintained very high catalytic activity after five reuse cycles. In addition, the reported materials were compared with these works, as shown in Table. S1, and the Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs exhibited excellent degradation rate and cycling stability compared with these materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of TeCG degradation using Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 , the reaction kinetics of TeCG over catalysts with different factors were calculated using a pseudo-first-order kinetic model (−ln(C/C0) = kt), where C and C0 are the concentration at certain intervals of time and the initial concentration, respectively, and k is the first-order rate constant. The maximum rate constant during the experiment was 0.03144 min−1, and the results showed that this catalyst could remove TeCG efficiently under suitable conditions. The fitting results showed that the degradation of TeCG by Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQD catalysts conformed to the pseudo-primary kinetic equation [45].

The plot of ln k against 1000/T a linear relationship is shown in Fig. 6f. Based on the Arrhenius equation (lnk = -1000Ea/RT + C), the reaction activation energy for the catalytic degradation of TeCG using Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs can be calculated to be about 35.41 kJ mol−1. The relatively low activation energy indicates that the degradation of TeCG using Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs is effective in solution, which is related to the intercalation of GQDs. First, GQDs possess high crystallinity and rich edge regions that can accelerate the oxidation reaction. Intercalation of GQDs into layers of LHDs improved the electric conductivity of nano-Fe3O4, leading to higher current density and electro-catalytic activity for Fenton’s reaction [46]. Second, GQDs have large specific surfaces. As a result, the large specific enhanced the sorption of the TeCG and H2O2, making Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs more thermally stable. Finally, the formed active dual-reaction center by virtue of GQDs accelerated the transfer of electrons provided by the TeCG to the hydroxyl radicals HO• center. All these factors significantly reduce the activation energy of the Fenton reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism of heterogeneous Fenton reagent
ce:section.ce:para: GC–MS was used to determine the degradation products of TeCG using Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs and H2O2. The MS of intermediate products are shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, and mass spectra data of intermediate products are listed in Table 1 .

As shown in Fig. S7, phthalic acid (C8H6O4) displayed a parent molecular ion peak at m/z 166.94(M + H+), strong molecular fragments at m/z 148.91(M−OH), and weak molecular fragments at m/z 131.93(M−2OH). In addition, phthalic acid displayed weak fragment ions at m/z 113.83(C8H17 + H+), fragment ions at m/z 82.97(C6H10 + H+), fragment ions at m/z 70.97 (C5H10 + H+), and fragment ions at m/z 56.93(C4H8 + H+).

As shown in Fig. S8, diisooctyl maleate [C4H2O4(C8H17)2] exhibited molecular fragments at m/z 211 (M−C8H17) and strong fragment ions at m/z 112.00(C8H17-H+), 99.88(C7H15 + H+), and 69.97(C5H11-H+). It also displayed molecular fragments at m/z 98.87(C7H15), 56.94(C4H9), and 42.94(C3H7). In addition, diisooctyl maleate displayed a fragment ion at m/z 82.97(C4H2O2 + H+).
All these results reveal the presence of final products (phthalic acid and diisooctyl maleate). The possible pathway of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQD catalytic degradation reaction to TeCG is shown in Fig. 7 .

According to the relevant literature, the reaction mechanism for the degradation of TeCG using a Fenton reagent consisting of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHS/GQDs and H2O2 is as follows (a schematic of the reaction mechanism is shown in Fig. 8 ): Mills and Hong believed that the degradation of organochlorides focuses on the process of removing chlorine atoms [47], especially polychlorinated compounds,which are generally dechlorinated into low-chlorine compounds in the presence of active free radicals such as hydroxyl radicals [48].The final product is phenol when TeCG is dechlorinated. Then, the phenol further produces cyclohexanol and 2-olefin cyclohexanol under the reduction of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs. Both of the two bi-reduction products continue to undergo ring cleavage after the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide through the linking reaction, producing 2-isooctanol and butanol.

Second, phenol with free radicals is catalyzed by Mg/Al/Fe-LDHS/GQDs, part of the benzene ring is cracked into butadiene, and part of the benzene ring is rearranged to generate active benzyne, which can react with butadiene to generate naphthalene. Naphthalene is oxidized to form phthalic acid by H2O2 [49]. The intermediate phenol produces maleic acid under the oxidation of H2O2, and maleic acid is esterified with isooctyl alcohol to produce diisooctyl maleate under the catalysis of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs.

In this study, novel Fenton catalysts Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs prepared via a hydrothermal method were used for the removal of TeCG, the effects of different catalyst systems, Fe content, H2O2 concentration, pH and temperature on the removal rate were studied. The results showed that the introduction of GQDs can form active double reaction centers on the surface of the catalyst through accelerating the transfer of electrons from TeCG to HO· charge centers, leading to significantly decreasing the activation energy of the Fenton reaction, therefore increased the degradation of TeCG by Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs. The simulated degradation experiments revealed that the maximum removal of TeCG by Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs was 89.34 % in 60 min under the optimal use conditions of H2O2 concentration of 60 mmol/L, pH = 3.0, and T = 45 °C. It was hypothesized that the possible reaction mechanism was that TeCG was first degraded to chlorophenol in the presence of Mg/Al/Fe-LDHs/GQDs and H2O2, followed by dechlorination to form phenol. The phenol then undergoes a ring-splitting reaction to produce a low or nontoxic end product, and the results of GC–MS testing compound this inference. The present study has achieved good results in TeCG degradation, but there are still challenges in the large-scale application of the catalyst and multi-pollutant treatment. Future research can further expand the catalysts in various directions, such as catalyst adaptability and engineering application, to promote the wide application of these catalysts in environmental remediation.
